name: World Politics
description: A core course examining the actors, structures, and dynamics of international relations, from state power and sovereignty to globalization, conflict, diplomacy, and contemporary global challenges.

modules.title: Introduction to International Relations
modules.objectives: Define international relations as a field of study; identify its core actors, levels of analysis, and major theoretical traditions; explain why world politics matters for everyday life.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 1.1: What is International Relations?
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Use the three levels of analysis (individual, state, systemic) to explain one recent international event
modules.assignment: Write a 350-word essay introducing international relations. Choose one current world news story and explain how it illustrates at least two key themes in the field (e.g., power, cooperation, conflict, sovereignty, or interdependence). Include one concrete example from the last two years. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Introduction to International Relations module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Nation-States & Sovereignty
modules.objectives: Define the nation-state and explain the origins of the modern state system; distinguish between nation, state, and nation-state; describe the concept of sovereignty and how it is challenged today.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 2.1: The Nation-State
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Identify three contemporary cases where state sovereignty is contested and explain the competing claims
modules.assignment: Select a contested territory or breakaway region (e.g., Taiwan, Kosovo, Western Sahara, or Crimea). Write a 350-word analysis explaining: (1) why that territory's status is disputed, (2) what arguments each side makes using the concept of sovereignty, and (3) your assessment of the strongest argument. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Nation-States & Sovereignty module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Realism & Liberalism in IR
modules.objectives: Summarize realist and liberal theoretical frameworks in international relations; compare their assumptions about human nature, state behavior, and the international system; apply each theory to a historical case.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 3.1–3.2: Realism and Liberalism
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Compare how a realist and a liberal would each explain why states go to war
modules.assignment: Choose either the 2003 US invasion of Iraq or NATO expansion into Eastern Europe. Write a 350-word essay analyzing the event using both a realist lens and a liberal lens. Clearly state which theoretical explanation you find more convincing and why. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Realism & Liberalism in IR module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: International Organizations (UN, NATO, WTO)
modules.titleZh: 国际组织：联合国、北约与世贸组织
modules.objectives: Describe the structure and purpose of major international organizations (UN, NATO, WTO, IMF); explain how intergovernmental organizations shape state behavior; assess both the capabilities and limitations of these bodies.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 7.1: International Organizations
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Identify one success and one failure of the UN Security Council and explain the structural reasons for each
modules.assignment: Research a specific UN peacekeeping mission or WTO trade dispute. In 350 words, describe what the international organization did, evaluate whether it was effective, and explain the key constraints (political, financial, or structural) it faced. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the International Organizations (UN, NATO, WTO) module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Globalization
modules.objectives: Define globalization and describe its economic, cultural, and political dimensions; distinguish between hyperglobalists, skeptics, and transformationalists; evaluate the benefits and costs of globalization for different populations.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 9.1: What is Globalization?
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: List two economic winners and two economic losers from globalization with supporting evidence
modules.assignment: Pick one product you use daily (smartphone, clothing, food item). Trace its global supply chain in 350 words: where are raw materials sourced, where is it manufactured, how does it reach you? Identify at least two ways globalization shaped this product's journey and one way globalization created inequality along the chain. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Globalization module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: International Trade & Finance
modules.objectives: Explain comparative advantage and the rationale for free trade; describe key institutions of the global economy (WTO, IMF, World Bank); identify causes and consequences of trade disputes and currency crises.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 10.1: International Trade
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Explain how a tariff affects domestic producers, domestic consumers, and trading partners using a concrete example
modules.assignment: Analyze the US-China trade dispute (2018–present) in 350 words. Explain: (1) the core economic and political grievances on each side, (2) the specific trade tools each government used (tariffs, sanctions, quotas), and (3) your assessment of whether the dispute was primarily about economics or geopolitics. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the International Trade & Finance module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Human Rights in Global Context
modules.objectives: Define human rights and trace the development of the international human rights regime; explain tensions between sovereignty and human rights intervention; evaluate the effectiveness of international human rights enforcement mechanisms.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 11.1: Human Rights
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Identify one case where a state's human rights record led to international intervention and evaluate the legal basis
modules.assignment: Choose an ongoing human rights crisis (e.g., Uyghurs in Xinjiang, Rohingya in Myanmar, or humanitarian situation in Gaza). Write 350 words: (1) describe the violations being documented, (2) explain what the international community has done (or failed to do), and (3) analyze the tension between sovereignty and the 'Responsibility to Protect' doctrine. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Human Rights in Global Context module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Causes of War & Conflict
modules.objectives: Identify and compare major theoretical explanations for war (realist, liberal, constructivist, domestic-level); distinguish between interstate and intrastate conflicts; explain why some disputes escalate to armed conflict while others do not.
modules.readingNote: University of Minnesota OER — International Relations Ch 5.1: Causes of War
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: Apply three different theories of war causation to explain the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine
modules.assignment: Select one major war since 1945 (Korean War, Vietnam War, Gulf War, or Russia-Ukraine). In 350 words, apply at least two theories of war causation to explain why it began. Evaluate which explanation is most persuasive and what it misses. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: sources, evidence, examples, maps, timelines, or analysis from the Causes of War & Conflict module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best defines 'international relations' as a field of study?
options: The study of domestic government institutions only
The study of interactions among states, international organizations, and non-state actors in the global arena
The study of cultural differences between countries
The study of international economic transactions
quizQuestions.answer: The study of interactions among states, international organizations, and non-state actors in the global arena
quizQuestions.explanation: IR broadly encompasses political, economic, security, and cultural interactions among states, international organizations (IGOs, NGOs), and multinational corporations.

quizQuestions.question: The 'systemic level of analysis' in IR focuses on:
options: Individual leaders and their personalities
Domestic political institutions
The structure of the international system as a whole
Military technology
quizQuestions.answer: The structure of the international system as a whole
quizQuestions.explanation: The systemic level examines how the anarchic structure of the international system and the distribution of power among states shapes behavior.

quizQuestions.question: In this course, which of the following best defines 'international relations' as a field of study?
options: The study of domestic government institutions only
The study of interactions among states, international organizations, and non-state actors in the global arena
The study of cultural differences between countries
The study of international economic transactions
quizQuestions.answer: The study of interactions among states, international organizations, and non-state actors in the global arena
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The study of interactions among states, international organizations, and non-state actors in the global arena.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which of the following is a primary reason everyday citizens should care about international relations?
quizQuestions.answer: International relations shape everyday security, prices, jobs, migration, rights, climate policy, and access to global goods.
quizQuestions.explanation: World politics is not only about diplomats; global decisions affect citizens through economics, security, rights, and environmental policy.

quizQuestions.question: Which component is essential for an entity to be recognized as a 'state' under international law?
options: A large military
A permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter relations
UN Security Council approval
A free-market economy
quizQuestions.answer: A permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter relations
quizQuestions.explanation: The Montevideo Convention (1933) defines statehood by four criteria: permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter into relations with other states.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'sovereignty' mean in international relations?
options: A state's right to exclusive authority within its borders and freedom from external interference
The power of the UN to govern member states
A state's military dominance over neighboring countries
The economic wealth of a nation
quizQuestions.answer: A state's right to exclusive authority within its borders and freedom from external interference
quizQuestions.explanation: Sovereignty refers to a state's supreme authority over its territory and population and its formal independence from external control.

quizQuestions.question: A 'nation' in the IR sense refers primarily to:
options: Any territory with defined borders
A group of people sharing common identity, culture, or history
Any member of the United Nations
An economically developed state
quizQuestions.answer: A group of people sharing common identity, culture, or history
quizQuestions.explanation: A nation is a group bound by shared identity (language, culture, ethnicity, history), which may or may not have its own state — hence the distinction between nation and state.

quizQuestions.question: In this course, which component is essential for an entity to be recognized as a 'state' under international law?
options: A large military
A permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter relations
UN Security Council approval
A free-market economy
quizQuestions.answer: A permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter relations
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: A permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter relations.

quizQuestions.question: Which assumption is central to the realist theory of international relations?
options: States are naturally cooperative and seek mutual benefit
International institutions effectively constrain state behavior
The international system is anarchic and states prioritize survival and power
Public opinion drives foreign policy
quizQuestions.answer: The international system is anarchic and states prioritize survival and power
quizQuestions.explanation: Realists argue the absence of a world government (anarchy) forces states to prioritize their own survival and power in a self-help system.

quizQuestions.question: Liberal theory in IR emphasizes that international cooperation is possible because:
options: States are irrational actors
International institutions, economic interdependence, and democracy reduce incentives for conflict
Military power is the only path to security
All states share identical interests
quizQuestions.answer: International institutions, economic interdependence, and democracy reduce incentives for conflict
quizQuestions.explanation: Liberals argue that institutions, trade, and democratic governance lower the costs and risks of cooperation, making peaceful relations possible even in an anarchic world.

quizQuestions.question: Hans Morgenthau is most associated with which theoretical tradition?
options: Constructivism
Liberalism
Classical Realism
Marxism
quizQuestions.answer: Classical Realism
quizQuestions.explanation: Morgenthau's Politics Among Nations (1948) is the foundational text of classical realism, arguing that states are driven by power and the national interest.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Constructivism in ir argues that state interests and identities are.
quizQuestions.answer: Socially constructed through ideas, norms, identities, and interactions rather than fixed only by material power.
quizQuestions.explanation: Constructivism argues that state interests and identities are shaped by shared meanings and norms.

quizQuestions.question: The United Nations was established primarily to:
options: Create a world government
Promote international peace, security, human rights, and cooperation among states
Regulate international trade
Enforce US foreign policy globally
quizQuestions.answer: Promote international peace, security, human rights, and cooperation among states
quizQuestions.explanation: The UN Charter (1945) established the organization to maintain international peace and security, develop friendly relations among nations, and promote human rights and international cooperation.

quizQuestions.question: Which UN body has primary responsibility for maintaining international peace and security?
options: UN General Assembly
UN Secretariat
UN Security Council
International Court of Justice
quizQuestions.answer: UN Security Council
quizQuestions.explanation: The UN Security Council (UNSC) has primary responsibility for peace and security under Chapter VII of the UN Charter, and its resolutions are binding on member states.

quizQuestions.question: In International Organizations (UN, NATO, WTO), NATO is best described as:
options: A global free trade organization
A military alliance among North American and European democracies based on collective defense
A humanitarian aid organization
An economic development fund for developing countries
quizQuestions.answer: A military alliance among North American and European democracies based on collective defense
quizQuestions.explanation: NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization) is a collective defense alliance; its founding principle is Article 5, which treats an attack on one member as an attack on all.

quizQuestions.question: The World Trade Organization (WTO) primarily:
options: Provides loans to developing countries
Sets rules for international trade and resolves trade disputes
Coordinates military operations
Monitors human rights violations
quizQuestions.answer: Sets rules for international trade and resolves trade disputes
quizQuestions.explanation: The WTO establishes international trade rules, reduces trade barriers, and provides a dispute settlement mechanism for member states.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best captures the meaning of 'globalization'?
options: The spread of American culture worldwide
The increasing interconnection of economies, cultures, and politics across national borders
The process by which all nations adopt the same government
The growth of military alliances
quizQuestions.answer: The increasing interconnection of economies, cultures, and politics across national borders
quizQuestions.explanation: Globalization refers to the accelerating integration of markets, cultures, technologies, and governance across national boundaries.

quizQuestions.question: 'Hyperglobalists' argue that globalization:
options: Has had minimal impact on the world economy
Is creating a borderless global economy that is transforming or eroding state power
Benefits only developed countries
Is slowing down due to rising nationalism
quizQuestions.answer: Is creating a borderless global economy that is transforming or eroding state power
quizQuestions.explanation: Hyperglobalists believe globalization is producing a qualitatively new global economy in which national borders matter much less than they once did.

quizQuestions.question: In this course, which of the following best captures the meaning of 'globalization'?
options: The spread of American culture worldwide
The increasing interconnection of economies, cultures, and politics across national borders
The process by which all nations adopt the same government
The growth of military alliances
quizQuestions.answer: The increasing interconnection of economies, cultures, and politics across national borders
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The increasing interconnection of economies, cultures, and politics across national borders.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best completes this statement? 'Hyperglobalists' argue that globalization:
options: Has had minimal impact on the world economy
Is creating a borderless global economy that is transforming or eroding state power
Benefits only developed countries
Is slowing down due to rising nationalism
quizQuestions.answer: Is creating a borderless global economy that is transforming or eroding state power
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Is creating a borderless global economy that is transforming or eroding state power.

quizQuestions.question: The principle of 'comparative advantage' in international trade argues that:
options: Countries should produce everything they need domestically
Countries benefit by specializing in goods they can produce at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for others
Only large economies benefit from trade
Trade always increases inequality between nations
quizQuestions.answer: Countries benefit by specializing in goods they can produce at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for others
quizQuestions.explanation: Comparative advantage, developed by David Ricardo, holds that even if a country is less efficient at producing everything, it gains from specializing in its relatively least-costly production and trading.

quizQuestions.question: Which institution was created to provide short-term loans to countries facing balance-of-payments crises?
options: World Trade Organization
International Monetary Fund (IMF)
World Bank
OECD
quizQuestions.answer: International Monetary Fund (IMF)
quizQuestions.explanation: The IMF was established at Bretton Woods (1944) to stabilize international exchange rates and provide short-term financial assistance to countries with balance-of-payments problems.

quizQuestions.question: A 'tariff' is best defined as:
options: A ban on importing a specific good
A government tax on imported goods
A voluntary agreement between two countries to limit trade
A subsidy given to domestic exporters
quizQuestions.answer: A government tax on imported goods
quizQuestions.explanation: A tariff is a tax levied on imports, raising the price of foreign goods relative to domestic products and thereby protecting domestic industries.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best completes this statement? The principle of 'comparative advantage' in international trade argues that:
options: Countries should produce everything they need domestically
Countries benefit by specializing in goods they can produce at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for others
Only large economies benefit from trade
Trade always increases inequality between nations
quizQuestions.answer: Countries benefit by specializing in goods they can produce at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for others
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Countries benefit by specializing in goods they can produce at relatively lower opportunity cost and trading for others.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: The us-china trade war that escalated in 2018 primarily involved.
quizQuestions.answer: Tariffs and retaliatory tariffs tied to trade imbalances, intellectual property disputes, and industrial policy competition.
quizQuestions.explanation: The 2018 escalation centered on tariff measures and broader conflict over trade practices and technology competition.

quizQuestions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly in:
quizQuestions.explanation: The UDHR was adopted on December 10, 1948, and established a common standard of fundamental human rights for all peoples and nations.

quizQuestions.question: Human rights are described as 'universal' because:
options: They apply only to citizens of democratic states
They belong to all human beings regardless of nationality, ethnicity, religion, or other status
They must be ratified by each country's parliament to apply
They only protect political and civil rights, not economic or social rights
quizQuestions.answer: They belong to all human beings regardless of nationality, ethnicity, religion, or other status
quizQuestions.explanation: The universality principle holds that human rights apply to every person simply by virtue of being human, not contingent on citizenship or government approval.

quizQuestions.question: Which option best completes this statement? The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) was adopted by the UN General Assembly in:
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 1948. The explanation asks students to connect the answer with cause, evidence, or consequence rather than memorizing a term.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which of the following best characterizes the international human rights regime's main weakness?
quizQuestions.answer: Weak enforcement because compliance often depends on state consent, political will, and international pressure.
quizQuestions.explanation: International human rights norms are influential, but enforcement is limited when powerful states resist or institutions lack coercive authority.

quizQuestions.question: Which theoretical perspective would most emphasize the security dilemma as a cause of war?
options: Liberalism
Constructivism
Realism
Marxism
quizQuestions.answer: Realism
quizQuestions.explanation: Realists argue the security dilemma — where one state's defensive buildup makes others feel less secure, triggering arms races — is a structural cause of conflict inherent in anarchy.

quizQuestions.question: An 'intrastate conflict' is one that occurs:
options: Between two or more sovereign states
Within the borders of a single state (civil war)
Between a state and an international organization
Across multiple continents simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: Within the borders of a single state (civil war)
quizQuestions.explanation: Intrastate conflicts (civil wars, insurgencies) occur within a country's borders, often between a government and non-state armed groups, as opposed to interstate wars between countries.

quizQuestions.question: The 'diversionary war' theory suggests that leaders may use military conflict to:
options: Secure access to natural resources
Distract the public from domestic political problems and rally national support
Fulfill treaty obligations
Balance power against a rising rival
quizQuestions.answer: Distract the public from domestic political problems and rally national support
quizQuestions.explanation: Diversionary war theory holds that leaders facing domestic unrest or declining popularity may initiate external conflict to boost nationalist sentiment and deflect attention from internal problems.

quizQuestions.question: In this course, which theoretical perspective would most emphasize the security dilemma as a cause of war?
options: Liberalism
Constructivism
Realism
Marxism
quizQuestions.answer: Realism
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Realism. It ties the answer to the historical, civic, geographic, or economic concept being assessed.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines the field of international relations?
options: Study of domestic political systems only
Study of interactions among states and non-state actors in the global arena
Study of international law enforcement
Study of economic development in poor countries
questions.answer: Study of interactions among states and non-state actors in the global arena
questions.explanation: IR examines political, economic, security, and cultural interactions among states, international organizations, and non-state actors across national borders.

questions.question: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) is significant in IR because it:
options: Created the United Nations
Established the principle of state sovereignty as the foundation of the international order
Ended World War II
Created the first international trade agreement
questions.answer: Established the principle of state sovereignty as the foundation of the international order
questions.explanation: Westphalia established that rulers had exclusive authority within their territories, founding the modern state-centric international system.

questions.question: The four Montevideo criteria for statehood include permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and:
options: UN membership
Nuclear weapons
Capacity to enter into relations with other states
A democratic constitution
questions.answer: Capacity to enter into relations with other states
questions.explanation: The Montevideo Convention (1933) defines statehood by these four criteria, the last being the capacity to conduct international relations.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement? Realism in IR assumes that the international system is characterized by:
options: Anarchy, state self-help, and competition for power or security
A world government with complete enforcement power
Permanent harmony among all states
The disappearance of national interest
questions.answer: Anarchy, state self-help, and competition for power or security
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Anarchy, state self-help, and competition for power or security.

questions.question: Liberal IR theory emphasizes which of the following as a source of international peace?
options: Military balancing and deterrence
Economic interdependence, democracy, and international institutions
The natural harmony between great powers
Cultural homogeneity among states
questions.answer: Economic interdependence, democracy, and international institutions
questions.explanation: Liberals argue that trade, democratic governance, and institutions create shared interests and reduce the incentive for conflict.

questions.question: The UN Security Council's five permanent members each possess:
options: The ability to deploy unlimited peacekeeping troops unilaterally
Veto power over substantive resolutions
The authority to amend the UN Charter alone
Special trade privileges under WTO rules
questions.answer: Veto power over substantive resolutions
questions.explanation: The P5 veto allows any permanent member (US, UK, France, Russia, China) to block any substantive UNSC resolution, often paralyzing collective action.

questions.question: NATO's founding collective defense principle (Article 5) states that:
questions.answer: An armed attack against one member is treated as an attack against all members. A strong response should use World Politics midterm concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.
questions.explanation: Article 5 is the core collective defense commitment of NATO.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement? Globalization's 'skeptics' (as defined by Held and McGrew) argue that:
options: Globalization is often overstated and states remain powerful actors
Globalization is unprecedented and fully eroding state power
International trade has never existed before
States have no role in regulating markets
questions.answer: Globalization is often overstated and states remain powerful actors
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Globalization is often overstated and states remain powerful actors.

questions.question: In global economic governance, the World Trade Organization (WTO) primarily:
options: Provides humanitarian assistance
Sets global trade rules and adjudicates trade disputes
Finances infrastructure in developing countries
Coordinates military alliances
questions.answer: Sets global trade rules and adjudicates trade disputes
questions.explanation: The WTO establishes trade rules, facilitates trade negotiations, and provides a binding dispute settlement system for its 164+ member states.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of 'soft power' in international relations?
options: The US imposing sanctions on Iran
China deploying warships in the South China Sea
South Korean pop culture (K-pop) building a favorable image of Korea globally
NATO conducting military exercises in Eastern Europe
questions.answer: South Korean pop culture (K-pop) building a favorable image of Korea globally
questions.explanation: Soft power works through cultural attraction and positive image; K-pop's global popularity enhances South Korea's international reputation without coercion.

questions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights is:
options: A legally binding treaty that states must implement
A non-binding declaration adopted by the UN General Assembly in 1948 establishing universal human rights standards
A document signed only by Western democracies
A military agreement for protecting civilians in wartime
questions.answer: A non-binding declaration adopted by the UN General Assembly in 1948 establishing universal human rights standards
questions.explanation: The UDHR is a declaration, not a treaty — it is not legally binding per se, but it has enormous moral authority and has shaped subsequent binding human rights treaties.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement? The 'security dilemma' in international relations refers to:
options: One state's defensive actions making other states feel less secure, which can trigger escalation
A treaty that permanently ends arms competition
A rule that only small states can join alliances
An economic policy that lowers tariffs
questions.answer: One state's defensive actions making other states feel less secure, which can trigger escalation
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: One state's defensive actions making other states feel less secure, which can trigger escalation.

questions.question: The Cuban Missile Crisis was resolved primarily through:
options: US military strikes against Soviet missile sites
US invasion of Cuba
Diplomatic negotiations leading to mutual concessions on missiles
A UN peacekeeping force deployed to Cuba
questions.answer: Diplomatic negotiations leading to mutual concessions on missiles
questions.explanation: The crisis ended diplomatically: the US promised not to invade Cuba (and secretly agreed to remove Jupiter missiles from Turkey), while the USSR withdrew its missiles from Cuba.

questions.question: A 'comparative advantage' in international trade means a country should specialize in goods:
questions.answer: It can produce at a lower opportunity cost than other countries. A strong response should use World Politics midterm concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.
questions.explanation: Comparative advantage is about opportunity cost, not absolute productivity.

questions.question: Constructivist IR theory argues that the interests of states are:
options: Fixed and determined by geography and power
Socially constructed through interaction, ideas, and shared norms
Solely determined by economic resources
Identical across all democratic states
questions.answer: Socially constructed through interaction, ideas, and shared norms
questions.explanation: Constructivists (e.g., Wendt) hold that identities and interests are not given by material factors but emerge from social interaction and the norms states develop in the international system.

questions.question: In this course, which level of analysis focuses on how the structure of the international system (anarchy, polarity) shapes state behavior?
options: System level
Individual level
Domestic interest-group level only
Personality level only
questions.answer: System level
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: System level. It ties the answer to the historical, civic, geographic, or economic concept being assessed.

questions.question: Which doctrine holds that the international community may intervene to protect civilians when a state commits mass atrocities?
options: Monroe Doctrine
Truman Doctrine
Responsibility to Protect (R2P)
Reagan Doctrine
questions.answer: Responsibility to Protect (R2P)
questions.explanation: R2P (adopted at the 2005 World Summit) holds that sovereignty entails responsibility; when states fail to protect their populations, the international community has the right to intervene.

questions.question: The Democratic Peace Theory predicts that:
options: All democracies are pacifist and never go to war
Democracies rarely fight each other, though they do fight non-democracies
Democracy leads to more international trade but not less conflict
Authoritarian states are less likely to initiate wars
questions.answer: Democracies rarely fight each other, though they do fight non-democracies
questions.explanation: The empirical pattern shows wars between established democracies are historically rare, while democracies do engage in wars with non-democratic states.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes China's Belt and Road Initiative?
options: A military alliance including China and its neighbors
A Chinese-funded infrastructure investment network spanning Asia, Africa, and Europe
A UN-sponsored development program
A free trade agreement between China and ASEAN
questions.answer: A Chinese-funded infrastructure investment network spanning Asia, Africa, and Europe
questions.explanation: The BRI involves Chinese financing of ports, railways, highways, and energy infrastructure in over 140 countries, extending Chinese economic influence and geopolitical reach.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement? The Paris Agreement's nationally determined contributions (NDCs) are:
options: Each country's self-defined climate action targets and commitments
Binding military quotas assigned by the UN Security Council
A list of private companies exempt from climate policy
Fixed tariffs on all fossil fuel imports
questions.answer: Each country's self-defined climate action targets and commitments
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Each country's self-defined climate action targets and commitments.

questions.question: 'Democratic backsliding' is best described as:
questions.answer: The gradual weakening of democratic institutions, rights, norms, and checks on power, often by elected leaders.
questions.explanation: Backsliding is usually incremental rather than a sudden military coup.

questions.question: The South China Sea dispute primarily involves China's claim to:
options: All islands and waters within its 'nine-dash line,' overlapping with the EEZs of multiple neighboring states
Military bases only, with no economic claims
Only the Spratly Islands, not the Paracel Islands
All waters east of Japan
questions.answer: All islands and waters within its 'nine-dash line,' overlapping with the EEZs of multiple neighboring states
questions.explanation: China's nine-dash line claims approximately 90% of the South China Sea, conflicting with the EEZs and territorial claims of Vietnam, the Philippines, Malaysia, Brunei, and Indonesia.

questions.question: The Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) is designed to:
options: Eliminate all nuclear weapons immediately
Prevent the spread of nuclear weapons while allowing peaceful nuclear energy use
Ban conventional weapons trade
Establish nuclear-weapon-free zones only in Europe
questions.answer: Prevent the spread of nuclear weapons while allowing peaceful nuclear energy use
questions.explanation: The NPT (1970) commits non-nuclear states not to acquire weapons, nuclear states to disarm eventually, and allows all signatories to pursue peaceful nuclear energy with IAEA safeguards.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement? A collective action problem arises when:
options: Individual incentives make it difficult for a group to achieve a shared goal
A state can solve a problem entirely on its own
All actors automatically cooperate without incentives
A problem has no shared consequences
questions.answer: Individual incentives make it difficult for a group to achieve a shared goal
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Individual incentives make it difficult for a group to achieve a shared goal.

questions.question: Economic sanctions imposed by the US on Russia following the 2022 invasion of Ukraine are an example of which foreign policy tool?
options: Military hard power
Economic coercive statecraft
Diplomatic recognition withdrawal
Humanitarian intervention
questions.answer: Economic coercive statecraft
questions.explanation: Economic sanctions (asset freezes, trade restrictions, financial exclusion) are coercive economic instruments used to impose costs on Russia without direct military engagement.

questions.question: Which international institution provides short-term loans to countries facing balance-of-payments crises?
options: World Bank
WTO
International Monetary Fund (IMF)
OECD
questions.answer: International Monetary Fund (IMF)
questions.explanation: The IMF was established at Bretton Woods to stabilize international monetary exchange and provide conditional emergency lending to countries with balance-of-payments difficulties.

questions.question: The Arab Spring (2010–2011) is best described in IR terms as:
options: A coordinated military coup organized by external powers
A wave of popular uprisings against authoritarian regimes across the Middle East and North Africa
A new economic integration initiative in the Arab world
A regional reaction to the 2008 global financial crisis only
questions.answer: A wave of popular uprisings against authoritarian regimes across the Middle East and North Africa
questions.explanation: The Arab Spring saw mass protests and uprisings in Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, Syria, Yemen, and elsewhere, driven by demands for democratic reform, human rights, and economic opportunity.

questions.question: Which framework best explains why states struggle to cooperate on controlling greenhouse gas emissions?
questions.answer: Collective action theory, especially the free-rider problem and tragedy of the commons.
questions.explanation: Climate mitigation creates shared benefits, so states may hope others bear the cost while they still benefit.

questions.question: Joseph Nye's concept of 'smart power' combines:
options: Nuclear and conventional military capabilities
Hard power (military and economic coercion) and soft power (attraction and legitimacy)
Bilateral and multilateral diplomacy
Economic development and military deterrence
questions.answer: Hard power (military and economic coercion) and soft power (attraction and legitimacy)
questions.explanation: Smart power is Nye's term for strategically combining hard power instruments (military force, sanctions) with soft power tools (cultural diplomacy, alliances, multilateral engagement).

questions.question: Which of the following is a major reason international human rights enforcement remains weak?
options: There are too few human rights treaties
Most human rights violations occur only in small countries
Sovereignty norms limit external interference, and no standing enforcement body has authority over powerful states
Human rights are not recognized in international law
questions.answer: Sovereignty norms limit external interference, and no standing enforcement body has authority over powerful states
questions.explanation: While international human rights law has developed substantially, enforcement is hampered by the sovereignty norm, P5 vetoes in the Security Council, and the lack of a powerful standing enforcement mechanism.

questions.question: The 'diversionary war' theory suggests military conflict may be initiated to:
options: Fulfill treaty obligations
Acquire natural resources from weaker states
Distract domestic audiences from internal political problems
Test new military technology
questions.answer: Distract domestic audiences from internal political problems
questions.explanation: Diversionary theory proposes that leaders facing domestic political pressure (economic crisis, scandal) may initiate international conflict to rally public support and shift attention outward.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement? ASEAN's principle of non-interference means that:
options: Members generally avoid intervening in one another's domestic political affairs
ASEAN has a common military command
Members must adopt a single currency
ASEAN directly appoints national leaders
questions.answer: Members generally avoid intervening in one another's domestic political affairs
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Members generally avoid intervening in one another's domestic political affairs.

questions.question: The International Criminal Court (ICC) has jurisdiction over:
options: Trade disputes between states
Territorial claims in international waters
Individuals accused of genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity
Violations of international environmental law
questions.answer: Individuals accused of genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity
questions.explanation: The ICC prosecutes individuals — not states — for the most serious international crimes, stepping in when national courts are unwilling or unable to do so.

questions.question: Which best characterizes the current international order (as of the 2020s)?
options: Firmly unipolar with unchallenged US dominance
Strictly bipolar with the US and China in an equal standoff
Increasingly multipolar with rising powers challenging US primacy
Fully multilateral with international institutions governing state behavior
questions.answer: Increasingly multipolar with rising powers challenging US primacy
questions.explanation: The post-Cold War unipolar moment is ending as China, India, the EU, and regional powers gain economic and political weight, creating a more contested, multipolar distribution of power.

questions.question: Which approach to global problems would a liberal institutionalist most strongly advocate?
questions.answer: Building international institutions, rules, agreements, and monitoring systems that help states cooperate.
questions.explanation: Liberal institutionalists argue that institutions reduce uncertainty and make cooperation more likely.
